content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

September 2, 1993

Honorable Daniel E. Boatwright
California State Senate
Room 3086, State Capitol
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-93-323

Dear Senator Boatwright:

This is in reply to your request for advice under the
Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ on behalf of Glen Ayers, a
Senate employee and consultant for the Senate Committee on
Business and Professions of which you are the Chairman.

QUESTION

Can a legislative staff member, who prior to being hired by
the Legislature was employed as an ordained minister, accept
payment after his date of hire for continuing his ministry and
conducting church services?

CONCLUSION

A legislative staff member who was employed as an ordained
minister prior to being hired by the Legislature may accept
payment for continuing his ministry and conducting church services
after his date of hire by the Legislature.

ANALYSIS

Section 89503 prohibits various designated employees of state
agencies, including the Legislature², from accepting honoraria.

¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000 et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

² See Section 82049, which includes the Legislature in the Act's
definition of "state agency."

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95812 • (916) 322-5660

File No. A-93-323
Page 2


Though you do not indicate in your letter, we assume that
Mr. Ayers is a designated employee of the Legislature.3

Section 89502(b), which is incorporated by reference into
Section 89503, defines "honorarium" as:

[A]ny payment made in consideration for any
speech given, article published, or
attendance at any public or private
conference, convention, meeting, social
event, meal or like gathering.

However, Section 89502(c), which is likewise incorporated by
reference into Section 89503, exempts from the definition of
honorarium "earned income for personal services which are
customarily provided in connection with the practice of a bona
fide business...." Commission Regulation 18932(a) clarifies that
this exception applies to the practice of or employment in a bona
fide business. Furthermore, Regulation 18932(b) clarifies that a
nonprofit entity, such as a church, is a "business" for purposes
of this exception.

Based upon these provisions, it is permissible for Mr. Ayers
to receive payments for continuing his ministry and conducting
church services while he is employed as a designated employee at
the Legislature. These services constitute the practice of, or
employment in, a bona fide business under Sections 89502 and
89503.4

Should you have any questions, please contact me at (916)
322-5901.

Sincerely,

Wayne Ordos
Executive Director

By: Scott Hallabrin
Assistant General Counsel

WO:SH:ak

3 If Mr. Ayers is not a designated employee, the honorarium ban
does not apply to him.

4 Please note that, under Section 89502(c), the "bona fide
business" exception does not apply to a business whose sole or
predominant activity is making speeches. We do not view this
provision as a problem in Mr. Ayers' case.

CONCLUSION: A legislative staff member who was employed as an ordained
